controls.name: Industrial DMZ Implementation
controls.description: Deploy a two-firewall Industrial DMZ between OT Level 3 and IT Level 4, breaking the direct IT-to-OT network path. All IT-OT data exchange flows through IDMZ-hosted services.
keyActivities: Deploy OT-side firewall (deny all, permit OT-to-IDMZ services)
Deploy IT-side firewall (deny all, permit IT-to-IDMZ services)
Migrate IT-OT data exchange to IDMZ services (historian mirror, patch server)
Document all permitted traffic flows

maturity.basic: VLAN separation between OT and IT; single firewall
maturity.mature: Full two-firewall IDMZ with deny-by-default rules; all IT-OT traffic through IDMZ services
maturity.advanced: Data diode for historian replication; OT-aware DPI; quarterly traffic flow review; zero direct IT-to-OT Layer 3 routes

controls.name: OT Remote Access Gateway
controls.description: Deploy a dedicated remote access gateway in the IDMZ as the sole path for all vendor and engineer remote access to OT systems. Enforce MFA, session recording, and per-session vendor approval.
keyActivities: Deploy OT remote access gateway (Secomea, Claroty SRA, Fortinet)
Enforce MFA for all remote access
Configure session recording
Implement per-session approval workflow for vendors

maturity.basic: VPN with password authentication
maturity.mature: Dedicated OT gateway with MFA; session logging; operator monitoring capability
maturity.advanced: Per-session vendor approval; session recording with 90-day retention; time-limited credentials; JIT access

controls.name: Passive OT Network Monitoring
controls.description: Deploy passive OT network monitoring sensors to capture and analyse all OT network traffic without sending any packets to control systems. Provides asset discovery, anomaly detection, and security alerting.
keyActivities: Deploy SPAN/TAP on OT network segments
Install OT monitoring sensor (Dragos, Claroty, Nozomi, Defender for IoT)
Establish asset and communication baseline
Configure OT-specific detection rules

maturity.basic: Monitoring deployed but limited coverage; manual alert review
maturity.mature: Full OT network coverage; automated alerting for key events; integration with SIEM
maturity.advanced: 24/7 SOC with OT expertise; AI/ML behavioural detection; real-time safety system anomaly detection

controls.name: OT Asset Inventory
controls.description: Maintain a comprehensive, current inventory of all OT assets including hardware, software/firmware versions, network addresses, zone assignment, and criticality classification.
keyActivities: Passive network discovery for initial asset identification
Manual walkdown for non-networked assets
Populate and maintain asset database
Quarterly reconciliation

maturity.basic: Manual spreadsheet asset list — best effort completeness
maturity.mature: Passive-discovery-driven automated inventory; quarterly review; asset change triggers update
maturity.advanced: Automated inventory with continuous reconciliation; alert on new/unregistered devices; hardware identity verification

controls.name: Default Credential Elimination
controls.description: Change all default vendor credentials on OT devices before network connection at commissioning. Audit all existing OT assets for default credentials. The single highest-impact, lowest-cost OT security action.
keyActivities: Create commissioning checklist requiring default credential change
Change defaults on all devices before OT network connection
Audit existing OT assets for default credentials annually
Store credentials only in PAM vault

maturity.basic: Default credentials changed on major assets; ad hoc approach
maturity.mature: Commissioning checklist enforced; all new assets; annual audit of existing assets
maturity.advanced: Automated detection of default credentials via OT monitoring platform; zero known default credentials in environment

controls.name: SIS Physical Network Isolation
controls.description: Physically isolate Safety Instrumented System controllers from all other networks. No network cables between SIS and DCS/HMI. Data diode for outbound SIS status data only. Hardware key switch required for engineering mode.
keyActivities: Physical cable trace to verify SIS isolation
Remove any DCS-SIS cross-connections
Install data diode for outbound SIS status
Enable hardware key switches on SIS controllers
Establish two-person rule for SIS work

maturity.basic: SIS on separate VLAN — still network-accessible
maturity.mature: Physical network separation; data diode; key switch enabled
maturity.advanced: Fully air-gapped SIS with data diode; dedicated isolated EWS; two-person rule enforced; annual physical inspection

controls.name: OT Patch Management Programme
controls.description: Structured OT patch management: vendor advisory subscriptions, applicability assessment, vendor qualification check, test environment validation, change management approval, maintenance window deployment, and compensating controls for unpatchable assets.
keyActivities: Subscribe to vendor security advisories
Assess each advisory against asset inventory
Vendor-qualification check before patching
Deploy via change management process

maturity.basic: Patches applied irregularly; no formal process
maturity.mature: Advisory subscriptions; vendor qualification check; quarterly patch deployment schedule
maturity.advanced: Test environment for patch validation; compensating controls for unpatchable assets with formal risk acceptance; biannual vulnerability assessment

controls.name: OT Configuration Backup and Recovery
controls.description: Automated backup of all OT configuration — PLC programs, DCS config, HMI, SCADA DB, historian, network devices. Offline copy. Quarterly restore test. OT-specific cyber recovery procedure.
keyActivities: Define backup scope covering all OT components
Automate scheduled backups
Store offline copy
Quarterly restore test
Develop OT cyber recovery procedure

maturity.basic: Manual backup periodically; no test
maturity.mature: Automated backup; offline copy; quarterly restore test; RTO defined
maturity.advanced: Immutable backup storage; cyber incident-specific recovery procedure tested annually; operations engineering integrated in recovery plan

controls.name: OT Change Management
controls.description: Formal change management process for all OT configuration changes: change request, technical review, operational approval, rollback plan, maintenance window deployment, post-change verification.
keyActivities: Define OT change management procedure
Require change requests for all OT modifications
Document rollback plans
Conduct post-change functional verification

maturity.basic: Some change tracking; ad hoc approval
maturity.mature: Formal change management; rollback plans; post-change verification; emergency change process
maturity.advanced: Automated change detection; configuration monitoring alerts reconciled with change log; unauthorised change triggers incident

controls.name: OT Vendor Security Assessment
controls.description: Security questionnaire, assessment, and contractual requirements for all OT vendors, system integrators, and service providers. Approved vendor register with annual review.
keyActivities: Develop vendor security questionnaire
Assess all critical vendors
Include security requirements in OT contracts
Maintain approved vendor register

maturity.basic: Informal vendor assessment; no contract requirements
maturity.mature: Questionnaire for critical vendors; contractual security requirements; approved vendor register
maturity.advanced: Annual vendor reassessment; right-to-audit clauses; SBOM requirement for new products

controls.name: NACSA Incident Notification Procedure
controls.description: Documented and tested procedure for classifying OT incidents against NACSA s26 significant-impact threshold and notifying NACSA within 6 hours. Includes 72-hour follow-up and 30-day final report procedures.
keyActivities: Develop incident classification matrix
Define notification decision authority
Pre-populate notification templates
Test in tabletop exercises

maturity.basic: Awareness that notification is required; no tested procedure
maturity.mature: Documented procedure; NACSA contacts maintained; tested in tabletop
maturity.advanced: Automated alert triage support; duty officer 24/7; notification tested under simulated incident conditions; sub-6h demonstrated in exercises

controls.name: Multi-Factor Authentication for OT
controls.description: Enforce multi-factor authentication for all human access to OT systems. MFA prevents credential-based attacks — the primary OT initial access vector after remote access exploitation. Applies to remote access gateway, engineering workstation login, and SCADA/HMI admin access.
keyActivities: Deploy OT remote access gateway with MFA enforcement (hardware token, authenticator app, or smart card)
Enforce MFA at the IDMZ jump server for all privileged OT access
Configure Windows Hello for Business or smart card on OT engineering workstations
Document MFA exemptions for operator HMI terminals with operational justification and compensating controls
Test MFA lockout recovery procedure to ensure it does not block emergency access

maturity.basic: MFA deployed for remote access VPN; on-site OT access still password-only
maturity.mature: MFA enforced at IDMZ remote access gateway and on engineering workstations; operator HMIs on policy exemption with documented compensating controls
maturity.advanced: MFA on all OT human access; hardware tokens for privileged accounts; MFA integrated with PAM for just-in-time privilege elevation; safety system access requires hardware key + MFA

controls.name: Role-Based Access Control for OT
controls.description: Define and implement OT-specific roles with least-privilege access. OT RBAC must balance security with operational availability — operator access to process control, engineer access to programming, administrator access to security configuration. RBAC reduces the blast radius of account compromise.
keyActivities: Define OT role taxonomy: Operator, Engineer, SCADA Admin, Security Admin, Vendor/Contractor
Map each role to specific access rights per OT system (HMI, SCADA, historian, EWS, PAM)
Implement RBAC in SCADA/DCS/HMI user management and Active Directory where integrated
Restrict PLC programming access to Engineering Workstation Zone — Operator HMI cannot reach engineering functions
Quarterly review of Engineer and Admin role assignments

maturity.basic: Basic role separation (Operator vs Admin); informal assignment
maturity.mature: Defined RBAC with least privilege; all roles documented; quarterly privileged role review; vendor accounts most restricted by default
maturity.advanced: Fine-grained RBAC; separation of duties for safety-critical changes; PAM for all privilege escalation; dual-authorisation for safety system configuration changes

controls.name: OT Zone Physical Access Control
controls.description: Control physical access to OT control rooms, server rooms, relay rooms, and MCC rooms. Physical access is the most direct path to OT compromise — an attacker with physical access to a PLC can reprogram it regardless of network controls.
keyActivities: Install electronic access control (card reader / PIN) on all OT control rooms, server rooms, and relay/MCC rooms
Retain access logs for minimum 90 days; review monthly
Remove access for personnel who have changed roles or left within 24 hours
Implement anti-tailgating controls in high-security OT areas (mantrap, security guard, camera review)
Conduct annual physical access list review — remove all personnel who no longer require access

maturity.basic: Mechanical locks on OT rooms; key holder list maintained
maturity.mature: Electronic access control with audit log; access list reviewed annually; visitors escorted; any personnel change triggers access review
maturity.advanced: Mantrap or security guard for highest-security OT areas; biometric access for safety system rooms; real-time access anomaly detection; integration with HR for automated access revocation

controls.name: OT Equipment Tamper Detection
controls.description: Deploy tamper-evident and tamper-detection controls on critical OT equipment — PLCs, RTUs, safety system controllers, and field cabinets. Physical tampering enables hardware implants, firmware modification, and bypass of logical security controls.
keyActivities: Apply tamper-evident seals to PLC chassis, RTU enclosures, and SIS controller panels
Lock field cabinets with uniquely keyed locks — not shared keys
Inspect tamper seals at every maintenance visit; document and investigate any broken seal
Configure chassis intrusion detection on OT servers and workstations where available
Photograph OT equipment at commissioning and after each maintenance visit — baseline documentation

maturity.basic: Key locks on field cabinets; basic seal on critical equipment
maturity.mature: Tamper-evident seals documented with IDs; inspection at each maintenance visit; broken seal triggers investigation; chassis intrusion detection on OT servers
maturity.advanced: Hardware security modules with physical tamper detection; anti-tamper enclosures on highest-criticality PLCs/RTUs; camera coverage on SIS controller rooms; any tamper event triggers immediate security response

controls.name: OT Data Encryption (Transit and Rest)
controls.description: Encrypt sensitive OT data in transit (remote access sessions, inter-zone communications, historian replication) and at rest (portable media, backup files, sensitive configuration documents). Encryption protects against interception and data exfiltration from OT environments.
keyActivities: Enforce TLS 1.2+ (TLS 1.3 preferred) on all remote access sessions and inter-zone communications
Configure historian replication over encrypted tunnel (IPsec or TLS) — not plain TCP
Encrypt all portable media used to carry OT data (BitLocker, VeraCrypt)
Disable weak cipher suites (RC4, DES, 3DES, TLS 1.0/1.1) on OT servers and remote access gateways
Implement certificate lifecycle management — expiry monitoring and renewal procedure

maturity.basic: TLS for remote access; portable media encryption policy
maturity.mature: TLS 1.2+ enforced for all inter-zone communications; weak ciphers disabled; portable media technically enforced; certificate expiry monitoring
maturity.advanced: TLS 1.3 preferred; OT PKI for device authentication; HSM for key management; post-quantum cryptography roadmap; all OT data at rest encrypted on servers

controls.name: Historian and Process Data Integrity
controls.description: Protect OT historian and process data from unauthorised modification. Historian data is critical for operational decisions, incident investigation, and NACSA s26 evidence. Unauthorised modification undermines all post-incident analysis.
keyActivities: Configure historian write access to SCADA/DCS data bus only — human users read-only by default
Enable historian access logging (reads and writes)
Ensure historian database is accessible from IT network only via IDMZ proxy, not directly
Implement historian backup with integrity verification (hash or checksum)
Restrict bulk historian data export — require authorisation and log all exports

maturity.basic: Historian access logged; write access restricted to SCADA data bus
maturity.mature: RBAC enforced on historian; access logging enabled; backup with integrity verification; IDMZ proxy for IT access
maturity.advanced: Automated integrity monitoring; alert on any unexpected write; cryptographic integrity on historian backup files; any integrity violation triggers immediate incident investigation

controls.name: OT Password-Based Authentication Policy
controls.description: Establish and enforce password policies tailored to OT environments, covering complexity, rotation, lockout, and shared-account management. OT password policies must balance security with operational availability — overly aggressive lockout on an operator HMI can impede emergency response.
keyActivities: Define OT password complexity requirements (minimum 12 characters, mix of character types) for all human-interactive accounts
Implement account lockout after 5 failed attempts with automatic unlock after 15 minutes — do not permanently lock operator accounts
Enforce password rotation every 180 days for privileged accounts; 365 days for operator accounts with compensating controls
Eliminate shared/group passwords — assign individual accounts where technically feasible; document shared account exceptions with compensating controls (session recording, physical access log)
Store all OT passwords in PAM vault — prohibit written/posted passwords in control rooms

maturity.basic: Password complexity enforced on domain-joined OT assets; standalone devices still use simple or shared passwords
maturity.mature: Password policy enforced across all OT assets; shared account exceptions documented with compensating controls; PAM vault for all credentials; lockout tuned per device criticality
maturity.advanced: Passwordless authentication (smart card, certificate) for privileged OT access; automated credential rotation via PAM; continuous monitoring for password policy violations; breach-password detection integrated

controls.name: OT Device-to-Device Authentication
controls.description: Authenticate OT devices before permitting communication — PLC-to-PLC, PLC-to-historian, SCADA-to-RTU, and engineering workstation-to-controller. Prevents rogue device injection and man-in-the-middle attacks on industrial protocols.
keyActivities: Inventory all device-to-device communication paths in the OT environment
Enable protocol-level authentication where supported (OPC UA mutual TLS, Modbus/TCP with TLS wrapper, DNP3 Secure Authentication)
Deploy certificate-based device identity for new installations — OT PKI or vendor-managed certificates
Configure allowlists on PLCs and controllers to accept connections only from known source IP/MAC pairs as compensating control where protocol-level auth is unavailable
Monitor for unauthenticated device connections via passive OT network monitoring

maturity.basic: IP/MAC allowlisting on critical controllers; legacy protocols unauthenticated with network segmentation as compensating control
maturity.mature: Protocol-level authentication enabled where supported (OPC UA, DNP3-SA); certificate-based identity for new deployments; unauthenticated connections monitored and alerted
maturity.advanced: OT PKI with automated certificate lifecycle; mutual TLS for all inter-zone device communication; hardware root of trust for device identity; rogue device detection within 5 minutes

controls.name: OT Account Lifecycle Management
controls.description: Manage the full lifecycle of OT accounts — provisioning, periodic review, role change, and deprovisioning. Orphaned accounts on OT systems are a persistent attack vector because OT devices are rarely rebuilt or reimaged, accumulating stale accounts over decades of operation.
keyActivities: Define OT account provisioning procedure requiring manager approval and documented business justification
Integrate OT account provisioning with HR onboarding/offboarding where feasible; manual process with SLA for standalone OT systems
Disable accounts within 24 hours of personnel departure or role change — same-day for privileged accounts
Conduct quarterly review of all OT accounts on SCADA, DCS, historian, engineering workstations, and domain controllers
Identify and remediate orphaned accounts on standalone OT devices (PLCs, HMIs, network switches) during annual maintenance

maturity.basic: Account provisioning via request; deprovisioning ad hoc; annual account review on domain-joined assets
maturity.mature: Formal provisioning/deprovisioning procedure with SLA; quarterly account review across all OT systems; orphaned accounts identified and removed; vendor/contractor accounts time-limited by default
maturity.advanced: Automated provisioning/deprovisioning integrated with HR system; continuous account monitoring — alert on dormant accounts; just-in-time access for all privileged OT accounts; zero standing privileges for vendor accounts

controls.name: OT Input Validation and Command Integrity
controls.description: Validate all inputs to OT systems — setpoint commands, configuration uploads, firmware images, and inter-system data transfers. Prevents command injection, out-of-range setpoint manipulation, and malicious firmware upload that could cause physical process disruption or safety incidents.
keyActivities: Configure setpoint range limits on PLCs and DCS — reject commands outside safe operating envelope
Enable rate-of-change limits on critical process variables to prevent sudden unsafe state transitions
Validate firmware images via vendor-signed hash before upload to any OT device
Implement application-layer firewalling (OT-aware DPI) to inspect and validate industrial protocol commands at zone boundaries
Log all rejected or out-of-range commands for security investigation

maturity.basic: Setpoint limits configured on safety-critical loops; firmware validated manually before upload
maturity.mature: Setpoint and rate-of-change limits on all critical process variables; OT-aware DPI at zone boundaries; firmware signing enforced; rejected commands logged and reviewed
maturity.advanced: Machine learning-based command anomaly detection; automated blocking of anomalous setpoint changes; cryptographic firmware signing with hardware root of trust; all industrial protocol commands validated at zone boundary

controls.name: OT Access Control Enforcement and Non-Repudiation
controls.description: Enforce access control decisions at the system level and provide non-repudiation — every action in the OT environment is attributable to a specific individual. Critical for incident investigation, regulatory compliance, and deterrence of insider threats in operational technology environments.
keyActivities: Configure audit logging on all OT systems to capture user identity, action performed, timestamp, and source address
Eliminate shared/generic accounts on SCADA, DCS, and historian — each operator has individual credentials
Forward OT audit logs to centralised SIEM or OT log collector with tamper-resistant storage
Implement session recording for all privileged OT access (engineering workstation, SCADA admin, remote access)
Review OT audit logs weekly for anomalous access patterns — privilege escalation, off-hours access, unusual command sequences

maturity.basic: Audit logging enabled on major OT systems; shared accounts still in use on some HMIs; logs stored locally
maturity.mature: Individual accounts on all SCADA/DCS/historian systems; audit logs forwarded to SIEM; session recording for privileged access; weekly log review
maturity.advanced: Full non-repudiation across all OT access — no shared accounts; cryptographically signed audit logs; automated anomaly detection on access patterns; real-time alerting on privilege escalation; forensic-grade log retention (12+ months)

controls.name: OT Session Management
controls.description: Manage active sessions on OT systems — enforce timeouts, limit concurrent sessions, and lock inactive sessions. Unattended logged-in sessions on engineering workstations and SCADA terminals are a direct path to OT compromise without requiring credential theft.
keyActivities: Configure automatic session lock after 15 minutes of inactivity on engineering workstations and SCADA admin consoles
Define session timeout exceptions for operator HMI terminals with documented operational justification and compensating controls (physical access control, camera coverage)
Limit concurrent sessions per user — maximum 1 active session for privileged accounts; prevent credential sharing detection
Enforce automatic session termination for remote access after 8 hours maximum duration — require re-authentication
Log all session events (login, logout, lock, timeout, concurrent session denial) to SIEM

maturity.basic: Screen lock configured on engineering workstations; remote access timeout enabled; operator HMIs may remain logged in
maturity.mature: Session lock on all non-operator OT systems; concurrent session limits for privileged accounts; remote access session duration limit; operator HMI exemptions documented with compensating controls
maturity.advanced: Adaptive session timeouts based on risk context; continuous session monitoring with anomaly detection; automatic session termination on suspicious activity; zero persistent sessions for privileged accounts

controls.name: Emergency Power for Critical OT Systems
controls.description: Provide uninterruptible and emergency power to critical OT systems — safety instrumented systems, SCADA servers, communication infrastructure, and security systems. Power loss can cause uncontrolled process shutdowns, loss of visibility, and disable security controls simultaneously.
keyActivities: Deploy UPS on all critical OT systems — SIS controllers, SCADA servers, network infrastructure, historian, and security appliances
Size UPS to provide minimum 30 minutes runtime for orderly shutdown or 4 hours for ride-through until generator start
Install generator backup for OT control rooms and server rooms with automatic transfer switch (ATS)
Test UPS under load quarterly; test generator with ATS semi-annually; document results
Define and test graceful OT shutdown procedure for extended power loss scenarios

maturity.basic: UPS on SCADA servers and SIS controllers; generator available but manual start; untested shutdown procedure
maturity.mature: UPS on all critical OT systems sized for 30+ minutes; generator with ATS; quarterly UPS load test; semi-annual generator test; documented graceful shutdown procedure
maturity.advanced: Redundant UPS with N+1 configuration; dual-feed power to critical OT racks; automated power monitoring with predictive battery failure alerting; annual full-facility power loss exercise including OT graceful shutdown and recovery

controls.name: OT Unnecessary Service and Port Elimination
controls.description: Minimise the attack surface of OT systems by disabling unused services, closing unnecessary ports, and removing unneeded software. OT devices often ship with services enabled by default (web servers, SNMP, Telnet, FTP) that are never used operationally but provide attack vectors.
keyActivities: Inventory all enabled services and open ports on each OT asset class (PLC, HMI, SCADA server, historian, network device)
Define a hardening baseline per asset class — list of required services and ports; disable or block everything else
Disable unnecessary services on OT devices: Telnet, FTP, unused web servers, SNMP v1/v2, unused industrial protocol listeners
Apply host-based firewall rules on Windows/Linux OT servers to block all non-essential inbound ports
Validate hardening baseline compliance after each maintenance activity and during quarterly reviews

maturity.basic: Known unnecessary services disabled on major OT servers; no formal baseline; ad hoc approach
maturity.mature: Hardening baseline defined per OT asset class; applied to all new deployments; quarterly compliance check; deviation documented with business justification
maturity.advanced: Automated hardening compliance monitoring; alert on any new service or open port; hardening baseline version-controlled and updated with each firmware/OS release; zero unnecessary services across entire OT environment
